speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 6. It's April 3, 1995. We're interviewing Isabella Leitner. The interviewer is Raquel Grunwald. The city is New York. Country is USA. Language is English. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Isabella, you were telling us about how the dog didn't pick up the scent of you and your sisters. Can you just go on about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the [GERMAN] passed us by. We-- and I guess my sister called out, after she must have seen from the attic the [GERMAN] had passed us by. And we went up to the attic, and we ate the frozen, gorgeous, wonderful cabbage. And suddenly, we heard the door crack ajar a little bit, and three miserable creatures who also had escaped were coming into the house. And we yelled down and said, nobody's here. You can come up. And then we attacked the [GERMAN], which is the pantry. And before Christmas, they slaughtered a pig, and it's all-- you know, in the smokehouse. And there is nothing but pig all over the place and stolen American cans. And the place is filled with food, and no-- but no bread. And we attacked all this food and nearly killed ourselves with it, as many people had died-- had died from overeating. And we ate and ate. And had a huge bin, and we used it as a bathroom, because there was no indoor bathroom. And-- and we ate and ate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before they will come and find us and shoot us, we wanted to eat, except my sister Chica, who laid down on a couch and was figuring out what we could do. And we were laughing at her, because why don't you eat? Why don't you use the little time you're living for eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Wednesday night-- this was Tuesday-- and Wednesday night, the lights went out. And my sister said it means the Germans left the area. There is something going on. There are no lights. And we ate and ate and used the so-called bathroom facilities. And we were getting very sick, I would imagine. I still had my typhus. I don't know how I did not die. And then Thursday morning-- and we lit a fire, and we were warm and happy and fed. And we were sleeping, and there were all kinds of wonderful clothing for a bride-to-be. They evidently had a daughter who was to be married, and they had all this dowry. And we were even trying on some of the clothes. We  were now not hungry anymore. And Thursday morning, Thursday about 1 o'clock, midday, we heard tremendous noises. And-- and we peaked out-- took away the kitchen curtain and peaked out. And there were bedraggled, bloody soldiers, and horses, and tanks, and tractors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we said, a whole army is coming to kill six skeletons. Why didn't you go home to your children and get loved and give love? Why do you do this? Why are you coming to kill us? And as we looked out and we looked some more, suddenly we couldn't tell what kind of soldiers they were. They were unfamiliar. They were not German. They were not Hungarian. Who are they? And suddenly we saw the red flag and the sickle and the-- what is it? Hammer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sickle and the hammer. And we were in some little cotton dress. We were under the covers. And there was this huge snowstorm, and we ran out barefooted and started to shriek. Six years of pent-up agony, a prison of-- of hatred.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started to shriek, we are free! We are free! We are free! And couldn't stop shouting we are free, until we finally said they are-- yes, but where are they all? They're  all dead. And  we shrieked. You could have heard it probably on 42nd Street. And-- and we were free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we stayed in-- in that town, in that house, for six weeks, because we had to take care of the cows. Because the only thing that they could save from this war-torn land was the cows that the Russians could take back to Russia and give milk to children to grow on. And we had to feed them. There was no other workhand, you know. They were all on the front. And there were tremendous fights in Breslau. We could hear the whole war from 50 kilometers away. And we took care of the cows, much as we did not know how to, but we did the best we could. We understood that we have to do it. And we also washed 60 shirts a day that came back from the front, a hard, canvas kind of shirts full of blood. And we had to wash it in lye.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had-- there was nobody else to do it. And the fights were as horrendous as maybe in Stalingrad, perhaps. There were tremendous fights in Breslau. And we washed the shirts in the lye, and we were crying, and-- and you know, our misery was unending somehow. But we had to wash the shirts. And finally after six weeks, they tried to make some order-- the Russians-- create some-- because nothing was left there, except they left their old mothers and fathers who couldn't walk. The Germans-- all the other Germans escaped. And we stayed there for six weeks, and they were very good to us. They-- they tore off my number from my neck, my prison number. You don't need this anymore. And they had some apples and bread on them, and they gave it to us immediately. But it was war, and it was hell. And it was a-- these were soldiers who fought for five years, six years, and they never had a furlough, unlike the Western armies. And when they went home finally, they found their homes burned up and their families murdered. And it was rough. It was hard. We understood, them. Luckily we were able to escape their advances, and we didn't get raped. There were tents, and some of them were caught in it, and others were able to escape it. As a matter of fact, related, in a more humorous way, when they came in the-- when they came in in the morning to get us to go to work, I was so afraid of getting raped that-- and by this time, I was quite lovely, because I gained weight, you know. And I would-- I would put on a torn socks full of holes on my shaved head, which was-- the hair was beginning to grow. And stuck out my tongue and put my eyes one eye up and one eye down and made believe that I can't walk straight, that I'm crippled, and had my hands in a funny way. Anything to escape being raped. And I did escape it. I made myself look like the Hunchback of Notre Dame. And we escaped it. And then we were put-- then we were told that we have to leave, because there is no more food in the area, and we have to make our way home. There was only one train in this war-torn land in a place called Oelsk. And we marched, as everybody did, for two weeks on the road, all the liberated people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we kept yelling, as everybody else did, have you seen my mother, my father, my sister, my brother? And every once in a while, we knew people who were with us in Auschwitz or from even my hometown. And we found out that this one is alive and that one is alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked for two weeks. And we reached Oelsk, and there, indeed, was a train. But it was a war-torn land of war machinery that was shot up probably, and it took us two weeks. All the liberated people from all over the world who were liberated by now, soldiers from every part of the world. And the Russians put us on these trains, and they made food for us whenever they can get-- could get some food. And the rails were so shot up that it took us two weeks to get to Odessa, and it was only 1,000 kilometers away. We probably should have made it  in less than a day, and it was two weeks. And we got there, and there was a big displacement center of some kind. And they ostensibly sent everybody home. Now, we wouldn't go home to Hungary. We knew there was nothing waiting for us there. And the only person who we are quite sure is alive is our father, and he's in America. And we want to go to America. And a British flyer who spoke Yiddish heard our story. And he was so moved by it that he immediately went to see the American attache or consul, or whoever he was, and told him our story. And we told him some of our story. and we were-- he made an appointment for us for the next morning at 9 o'clock, and he took the three of us there. And he was the trans-- he spoke Yiddish. And we told him the story in this fancy office of the American attache. And there were five people there, and everybody was crying. And we talked for hours. And he said, you'll be on the next boat going back to America. Because my father was here, and we legitimately could follow him. And they went-- and the next ship will leave in 16 days, and you'll be on that boat. And we went back to the center, and we said, oh, we're Americans! We're going to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now, and the attache was so moved that he went out to the docks, and he went onto the which he found out was leaving the very next day. And he said, captain-- who was not a kindly captain-- you'll take orders from me. You're taking back 16 liberated American soldiers and three Hungarian girls who survived Auschwitz. And the captain objected that it's not within his powers to do this. But the attache got in touch with America, with the FBI, and they cleared everything that, indeed, my father is here, and we're legitimately entitled to follow him. And he came back and knocked on the door where we were housed in Odessa and he said you're leaving tomorrow morning. And he brought two soldiers who packed our things. By this time, we acquired some clothing. And we went-- and the next morning at 8 o'clock, he was there, on April 6. And we climbed onto the Jeep, and he took us aboard the and he said, have a good trip. Fine. Goodbye. And I, for years, I don't know if I still do not-- didn't remember his name. And he left immediately. And we were at sea aboard the for nearly five weeks through Africa and God knows where. It was 4 and 1/2 weeks. And we arrived on the very day the war ended, on May 8, 1945, making us a very historic presence. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father reach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here, and we had a very-- we had to be housed. We arrived on a Thursday afternoon. We arrived originally on May 8 in Newport News, Virginia on a beautiful day, then it started to rain. And we arrived in-- the boat continued on to Baltimore, Maryland, and we arrived near the end of the day, which was Thursday. And we were taken off by an organization called-- whatever, some women. So the FBI took us off and took  us to the home of the most prominent citizen, who later became the Solicitor General of the United States, Simon E. Sobeloff. And he guaranteed-- you know, he took responsibility for us, and we were housed in his magnificent home. And he got on the telephone, and he tried to find a man named Menyhert Katz in New York, and he couldn't find him. He was a judge then, and he  had influence. And there was no way of finding a man listed Menyhert Katz in 166 Ross Street, Brooklyn. That's all we remembered, because that was my aunt's address, and he was living with his sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't find him. And finally, he got tired of the whole thing. And he told the operator, operator, you don't understand this. These three kids survived Auschwitz. You know what Auschwitz was? You know what Auschwitz is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they're here in America, and they're trying to find their father. And you've got to find a phone number at that address. And he says-- she said, there's no such listing. And then, finally, she said, there is a listing at that address for someone else. Well, go and get them immediately. And they got the lady who had a telephone, and they called on my aunt, and my aunt talked to us. And-- and then they went to get my father, and my father couldn't come to get us, because he was very religious. And those days, you took about seven hours to get by train to Baltimore. So he couldn't get there until Sunday morning. He started out Saturday night, and he got there Sunday morning. And we met my father. He was an old, broken man, and we were worlds apart, he with his extreme religiosity, and we with Auschwitz in our-- our skull. And it was very, very hard to find a common ground. It was very difficult, yet he took us back to New York. He paid for our fare. And my sister-- there was only one Jew, one Jewish officer on the ship, and that was Jack Berman. And they fell in love, and less than four weeks later, they got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicha. And they have three kids, and she has one grandchild and two on their way, and they're still married. And-- and then the two of us came to New York-- well, the three of us came to New York. And then she went off and she got married, and the two of us lived with my father. And then my father got a letter in May of 19-- after we were already in America. You know, the camps were liberated-- I don't know-- in April 15 or-- by the time we got word that my brother is alive, we were in America. And then my father brought my brother out, and he arrived in 1946 in January. And then one by one, we got married and had children, and here we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your husband? Whom did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a volunteer in the American Army. He was a flyer, a bomber. And he said, I tried to save you all. He bombed Berlin over and over again. And he, too, he's perhaps-- only this kind of a person can understand the kind of person that I am, and we have this great big war in common. Plus he-- you know, he is a writer, a New York-born writer who has earned his living from writing a variety of things and books and plays and children's books. And then finally, when our children were already grown and they were in-- in high school-- actually, before that, I begged him to write my story. And I don't know anyone who has the kind of integrity that my husband has, who said, I will-- I was not there. I tried to save you all, but I was not there. You have to write your book, and I will edit it and do whatever needs to be done with it. But you have to write it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I begged for years that he should do it. I didn't believe him, and he refused. And finally, one day I had to believe him. And I-- I sat down in front of the typewriter and began to write from these faded notes that I have that I've kept writing all along. And he put down his cup of coffee on the kitchen counter as I read, and he began to cry like a child. And he said, I told you only you can do it. And then-- and he said, just go ahead, and I'll do whatever needs to be done with it. And really, it was his opinion that mattered most to me, because he was the professional. And if he was satisfied, that was good enough for me to go on with. And I kept writing, to the point where one night I thought it was 3 o'clock in the morning, and I looked up and it was 7:00 in the morning. And I was still sitting at the typewriter. And as he promised, he did whatever needed to be done with it to put it in publishable form. And both our names appear on several books, because I went ahead and wrote a second book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, give us the name of your first book.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first book was called It came out in 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second-- of And the second one was called And then there was a third one, the children's book, which has found great understanding with the children throughout the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's called And it has my family's picture on it, which is very painful to me, to go into a bookstore and see my family displayed all over. I had to violate my sense of privacy in order to-- because I will only talk about what I know. What I know is my family and what happened to them, so it's very painful. Nobody likes it in my family, that the cover is the-- the family picture. But for all you bastards out there who dare to say anything about it, that it never happened-- there are such morons in this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look at them. These were not phantom people. This is my mother and my sisters. And how dare you say anything? And I have to display them like this for you to believe me, that these were not phantom people. These were real human beings, good human beings, much better than all those bastards out there. Did you have a fourth book? Is that the fourth one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was a fourth book. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm not sure it's the end yet, because I'm still filled with the misery inside. And the two books eventually went out of print, and-- and it should not have gone out of print. It was realized by Doubleday, Anchor Doubleday, that it must be in print again. And they took the both books and made them into one book. And it's called now And it is both books plus new material. So in a sense, it is a new book. There is additional material in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give us the name of your two sons and what they do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And our sons. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Peter, who is marvelous, wonderful, brilliant, cum laude, graduated Princeton University. And he chose alternate medicine, and he's very happy with it. And he does-- he's a chiropractor. He's married to a lovely girl who owns a gallery, and they're both artistic people. My son is a marvelous writer. So both my children are marvelous writers. And he's happy living out there. They have no children. I don't know what their plans are. I miss them terribly, because they live so far. And my son Richard, whom you just met, who is a-- a struggling actor. He does-- he's an actor and voice-over, and he chose this field. And I-- I am sometimes sad, because it-- it's so difficult a profession, but no one will deter him. And I hope that someday I  will see him all over on all the screens and hear his marvelous voice and look at his gorgeous face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I love them so terribly, I cannot believe. [SIGHS] I just can't believe that I gave birth to two children like this. It's so good. It's so wonderful. It's-- how can I believe this much grace from life? And they're the most glorious, gracious things that happened ever, ever to me. And I don't know how to find words for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just hope that they'll grow up in a world that will not know as much misery as I have known. And perhaps they are the ultimate reason for my working so hard. Keep writing and-- and lecturing, talking about it. Make sure that it's not forgotten, that it's remembered. And I do it at great-- it's great sacrifice. I'm too old to do as much as I'm doing. Yet I make new promises to myself that I will not do it anymore, and yet I find myself doing it anew again. And perhaps-- and my son Richard is now drawn into it, because he comes sometimes and does some reading from it, from my books. And I hurt for them, because it's not easy. It's not easy to be the children of a survivor of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Isabella. It was a total, total joy to speak with you. And you presented the story in a most articulate way. Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
